text: The surgeon begins the resection by performing an omentectomy, visually inspecting the liver and peritoneal surface for distant metastases before proceeding.

The surgeon identifies a thin rim of tissue between the third layer of the omentum and the transverse mesocolon, ensuring dissection in the correct plane to avoid damage to the mesocolon and its vessels. The video shows a clear demarcation between the yellow transverse mesocolon and the white third layer of the omentum in this relatively thin patient.

The surgeon performs a careful dissection near the head of the pancreas, where the gastroepiploic vein converges with the superior mesenteric vein and the loop of Henle is visible. The dissection is conducted gently to avoid bleeding.
The surgeon bares the right gastroepiploic vein of all lymphatic tissue and clips it at its root, followed by the right gastroepiploic artery, which is similarly bared and clipped at its root.
The surgeon dissects the final bit of tissue between the pedicle and the pylorus, ensuring all soft tissue remains with the specimen.
The surgeon incises the lesser omentum with an adequate margin from the stomach, coagulating the right gastric artery at its root while ensuring all lymphatic tissue is directed towards the specimen.

Following the division of the right gastroepiploic and right gastric pedicles, the surgeon has separated the gastroduodenal junction from surrounding soft tissues. The division of the lesser omentum is extended superiorly to the diaphragm's crus. The left gastric vein is dissected, with all surrounding soft tissue being removed and kept with the specimen, as the proximal gastric tumor involves the fundus and body.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in further dissection and proceeds to divide the stomach at its junction with the first part of the duodenum, preparing to separate these anatomical structures.

The surgeon bares the left gastric artery of all lymphatic tissue, pushing the lymphatic tissue towards the specimen. The origin of the left gastric artery is identified, and a clip is applied to secure it.

The surgeon uses a ligature to carefully separate flimsy adhesions between the stomach tumor and the capsule of the pancreas, ensuring no tumor tissue remains on the pancreas while avoiding injury to the pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon divides the last few adhesions between the stomach and the crura of the diaphragm, ensuring that all tissue affected by the chemotherapy reaction is included with the specimen.
The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels from the medial side of the greater curvature of the stomach, carefully navigating the anatomy to avoid injury to the spleen and prevent major bleeding.

A gauze piece is placed at the hilum of the spleen to keep it dry. The surgeon completes the mobilization of the stomach, ensuring a substantial amount of surrounding soft tissue and vessels are taken at their root. Following this, the lymph nodes along the common hepatic artery up to the right side of the right hepatic artery are removed.
